one_line_takeaway: A taxonomic survey that organizes the emerging landscape of attacks on large vision-language models into four categories (adversarial, jailbreak, prompt injection, data poisoning) and catalogs the associated datasets, victim models, metrics, and defenses, but contributes no new attack, defense, or experimental results of its own.
key_finding: See above; survey reports no original experimental numbers.
core_contribution: This is a survey paper that provides what the authors claim is the first comprehensive review of attacks specifically targeting Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). Its own contribution is organizational/taxonomic, not a new attack or defense: (1) a systematic taxonomy classifying existing LVLM attacks into four categories — adversarial attacks, jailbreak attacks, prompt injection attacks, and data poisoning/backdoor attacks; (2) a background/"preliminary" section formalizing what an LVLM attack is (notation for image x, text prompt t_in, output t_out, attack operations P(x)/P(t_in), perturbation budget epsilon, and targeted vs. untargeted optimization objectives), plus a catalog of attack tools, datasets, victim models, evaluation metrics, and defense strategies; and (3) a discussion of open challenges and six future research directions. The authors explicitly position their survey against three prior surveys (Chowdhury et al. on LLM text attacks, Liu et al. on multimodal LLM safety, Fan et al. on image-only attacks), arguing those have incomplete or coarse taxonomies. Attribution note: all specific attack/defense methods described (FGSM, JailGuard, ECSO, individual cited works) are prior work being catalogued, NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: The survey frames a general LVLM threat model rather than a single concrete one. Target: a victim LVLM M mapping (image x, text prompt t_in) to text output t_out, built from a visual encoder + adapter + LLM backbone. Adversary goal: apply attack operations P to one or both modalities so that M(P(x), P(t_in)) yields an attacker-chosen output t'_out, a jailbreak output containing harmful content, or (untargeted) any output deviating from the true one, subject to an imperceptibility constraint ||P||_p <= epsilon for adversarial attacks. Adversary capability is stratified into white-box (full access to architecture, parameters, gradients), gray-box (surrogate/partial knowledge), and black-box (query-only) settings; the survey notes that realistic deployed LVLM services expose no model details, so query-only black-box and transferable attacks are the practical frontier. Attack surface spans inference-time input manipulation (adversarial perturbations, jailbreak prompts, prompt injection) and training-time data poisoning/backdoor insertion.
limitations: As a survey it is inherently descriptive: it runs no experiments and provides no head-to-head empirical comparison of the surveyed attacks, and it explicitly flags (future direction 6) that existing methods use inconsistent models/datasets/metrics, so no unified quantitative ranking can be drawn from it. Coverage is a snapshot of roughly 2023-2024 work (arXiv v2, July 2024) and will date quickly. The taxonomy's four categories overlap in practice (e.g., jailbreak vs. prompt injection), and boundaries are somewhat coarse. Defense coverage is thinner than attack coverage — the defense table lists only about ten methods split into two broad types. The paper is a self-declared "first comprehensive" survey, a novelty claim that is not independently verifiable. No reproducibility artifacts beyond a curated GitHub reading list.

quantitative_results: No original experimental results — this is a survey; numbers below are catalog statistics, not measured performance
Attacks organized into 4 categories: adversarial, jailbreak, prompt injection, data poisoning/backdoor

13 representative open-source victim LVLMs compared by visual-encoder/adapter/LLM (Flamingo, BLIP-2, InstructBLIP, MiniGPT-4, MiniGPT-v2, LLaVA, LLaVA-1.5, OpenFlamingo, LLaMA-Adapter V2, PandaGPT, BLIVA, Qwen-VL, SPHINX-X)
3 attacker access settings tabulated: white-box (W), gray-box (G), black-box (B)
Primary effectiveness metric is Attack Success Rate (ASR = successful attacks / total attacks); Contain and Exactmatch used for targeted-text attacks
Rule-based eval metrics catalogued: F1, accuracy, BLEU-4, METEOR, CIDEr, SPICE, SSIM
Defenses split into 2 classes (inference-time vs training-time), ~10 methods tabulated e.g. JailGuard, ECSO
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines (survey). Prior surveys it positions against: Chowdhury et al. (Breaking Down the Defenses: attacks on LLMs, text-only)
Liu et al. (Safety of Multimodal LLMs on Images and Text — first LVLM safety survey, called incomplete/coarse)
Fan et al. (Unbridled Icarus: image-input perils in multimodal LLMs — image-only, misses textual prompt injection)
